summary: Gruber rebuts Christopher Mims's Quartz article claiming 2013 was a 'lost year for tech,' arguing it reflects a corrosive strain of cynicism in tech journalism. He methodically dismantles the 'planned obsolescence' narrative applied to Apple, showing that Apple supports more device generations than Google and that used iPhones retain far more resale value than competitors. He argues that real progress is incremental, not revolutionary, and that dismissing steady improvement as stagnation reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of how technology advances. The piece broadens into a critique of tech journalism's nihilistic tendency to tear down genuine achievement rather than evaluate it proportionally.
key_points: Technological progress is inherently incremental; the iPhone 5S was 40x faster than the original iPhone, achieved through yearly improvements cynics dismissed as unimpressive
The 'planned obsolescence' accusation against Apple is logically incoherent: if iOS updates made old phones worse, users would switch to competitors, not buy another iPhone
Apple supports four iPhone generations with iOS 7 while Google supports only two Nexus generations with Android 4.4, yet Apple gets accused of planned obsolescence
Used iPhones retain extraordinary resale value ($200-300 for 2-3 year old models), objectively disproving the claim that Apple deliberately degrades older devices
Tech journalism suffers from a unique nihilism where enthusiasts have no actual enthusiasm, unlike writers covering sports, food, cars, or other fields
money_quotes: What a sad pile of piss-on-everything cynicism.

The nature of progress is to move incrementally. The great leaps are exceedingly few and far between. One needs to pay attention, to learn to appreciate fine details, in order to appreciate progress as it churns.
If you're not excited by the performance of the A7 SoC or, say, the quality of the iPhone 5S camera, why even bother writing about technology?
It begins with a pernicious lie: that Apple somehow booby-traps its devices to malfunction after a certain too-brief period to spur upgrades to brand-new products.
If your car breaks down after just a few years, are you not more likely to replace it with a different brand?
Apple's business is making customers happy, and keeping them happy. They make products for discriminating people who have higher standards and less tolerance for design flaws or problems.
That which escapes the grasps of Sturgeon's Law and achieves genuine excellence deserves to be celebrated, not torn down through false equivalences and the sensational exaggeration of imperfections.

There's a nihilistic streak in tech journalism that I just don't see in other fields. Sports, movies, cars, wristwatches, cameras, food — writers who cover these fields tend to celebrate, to relish, the best their fields have to offer. Technology, on the other hand, seems to attract enthusiasts with no actual enthusiasm.
key_insight: Dismissing incremental technological progress as stagnation reveals not sophisticated criticism but a nihilistic inability to appreciate how real advancement works.
